title: From Forward Pass to Backward Pass: The Bridge
concept: The transition from the forward pass to the backward pass is bridged by the loss score, which serves as the starting point for backpropagation to calculate weight adjustments.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a common way to prevent log(0) NaN errors in cross-entropy calculation?

options.text: Add a tiny constant (epsilon) to the prediction inside the logarithm.

exercises.explain: Correct! A tiny safety constant prevents division-by-zero or log-of-zero math errors.

cards.title: The turning point
cards.body: When a climber scale a mountain, they reach the peak, take a breath, and look down. Reaching the peak is the forward pass; climbing down safely is the backward pass. The loss score is this peak — the turning point where we stop predicting and start learning. Let's look at how this bridge is crossed!

cards.title: The Gradient Hand-off
cards.body: The **forward pass** flows inputs -> predictions -> loss. Once the loss score is calculated, we run the pipeline in reverse. The **backward pass** (backpropagation) flows from the loss node back to the input, calculating gradients (error slopes) for every weight along the computation graph.

cards.title: The feedback loop in a business
cards.body: Think of a company. Step 1 (Forward): executives design a product and send it to customers (input to output). Step 2: customers report complaints (loss score). Step 3 (Backward): the complaints travel back through the departments, telling each team (layer) exactly what they need to fix to improve the product. The complaints (loss) are the bridge between action and correction.

cards.title: The starting gradient
cards.body: A model predicts `0.8` (target = `1.0`). The loss is `0.2`. The backward pass starts at this loss node. It calculates: 'How much would the loss change if our prediction changed slightly?' (the starting gradient). It then propagates this gradient backward through the computation graph, multiplying it by each layer's connection weights.

cards.title: Backward pass is mathematically linked to the forward pass
cards.body: You might think the backward pass runs on a completely different set of rules. In reality, backpropagation math (calculating derivatives) requires knowing the exact intermediate values calculated during the forward pass. This is why the computer must temporarily store (cache) the forward activations in RAM until the backward pass finishes.

cards.title: The Forward-Backward Loop
cards.body: How the two training phases connect:

left.text:
Inputs flow to output.
Calculates prediction.
Stores intermediate activations in RAM.

right.text:
Starts at loss node.
Uses stored activations.
Updates weights using gradients.

exercises.prompt: What serves as the starting point for the backward pass (backpropagation)?

options.text: The loss score calculated at the very end of the forward pass.
The raw inputs loaded in the first layer.

exercises.explain: Correct! Backpropagation starts at the loss node and flows backward.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The backward pass can run independently without needing any information about the intermediate values calculated during the forward pass.
exercises.explain: False! Backpropagation math requires the stored activations from the forward pass to calculate gradients.

exercises.sentence: The computer must temporarily store forward activations in ___ so the backward pass can use them to calculate gradients.
exercises.explain: Activations are stored in temporary RAM until the backward pass completes and weights are updated.

exercises.prompt: Which process describes the hand-off between the forward and backward passes?

options.text: The forward pass computes a loss of 1.20, which is instantly used by the backward pass to calculate the initial error gradient.
The model deletes its weights and starts retraining.
The forward pass outputs a prediction and the training loop stops.

exercises.explain: Correct! The calculated loss is the immediate input/starting point for the backward pass.

exercises.prompt: You are training a large language model. You increase the batch size from 32 to 128. Training runs faster, but the GPU throws an 'Out of Memory' error during the backward pass, not the forward pass. What caused this?

options.text: The larger batch size increased the size of the activation matrices that the GPU had to store in RAM during the forward pass to use during the backward pass, exceeding memory limits.
The backward pass does not use GPU memory.
The weights became physically larger during backpropagation.
The loss function was too complex.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Because backpropagation needs the forward activations to calculate gradients, the GPU must hold all those intermediate values in memory. Larger batches scale up these stored matrices, leading to memory crashes during the transition to the backward pass. Next, let's review everything we've built from logic gates to loss!
